content:
Looking back at my formative years, I was particularly **drawn to** History and Literature, as they provided a fascinating window into the complexities of human nature. While many of my peers found History to be a bit of a **dry subject**—primarily due to the focus on **rote memorization** of dates—I was captivated by the narratives of past civilizations. I found it incredibly rewarding to **unravel the cause-and-effect relationships** behind major global events, which helped me develop a more **nuanced understanding** of the world we live in today.

Similarly, Literature was a subject I truly **excelled in**. I had a particular **penchant for** analyzing classic novels and poetry, as it allowed for a high degree of **subjective interpretation** and critical thinking. There was something uniquely fulfilling about **delving into** the subtext of a story to uncover the author’s hidden messages. My teacher was exceptionally skilled at bringing these texts to life, which further **fueled my passion** for the written word.

In contrast, I must admit that I was never quite **computationally inclined**, so subjects like Physics or Chemistry often felt like an **uphill battle**. I much preferred the "soft sciences" where I could engage in debate and explore diverse perspectives. Ultimately, my time in school was defined by this love for the humanities, as these subjects not only **broadened my horizons** but also sharpened my **analytical prowess** in a way that continues to benefit me today.
